globals.name: cultivating_change_talk
globals.justification: The assistant consistently invited the user to explore their own motivations and reasons for wanting to improve their slides, such as asking what would need to happen for the user to feel more confident in their slides and academic path forward.

globals.name: softening_sustain_talk
globals.justification: The assistant skillfully reflected the user's concerns and fears without arguing against them, instead rolling with the user's resistance and honoring their autonomy, as seen in the response to the user's worry about a single mistake ruining their credibility.

globals.name: partnership
globals.justification: The assistant maintained a collaborative stance, working with the user to break down tasks into smaller steps and inviting their input on what would make them feel more confident, such as deciding which slide to tackle first.

globals.name: empathy
globals.justification: The assistant demonstrated a deep, nuanced understanding of the user's perspective, recognizing the weight of their fears and worries about not being good enough, and naming the underlying emotions and concerns that were driving their behavior, such as the fear of being seen as careless or incompetent.

evidence_citations.excerpt: every time I look at them I feel they're not good enough
I keep wondering if my research is novel enough—if it’s not, all this effort feels pointless
what if I still mess it up and the whole talk looks weak
I feel like the whole talk is a lie—reviewers will see me as careless, and I’ll doubt that my research even matters

updates.new_value: I am not good enough, I will be judged and found wanting, my work is not valuable

evidence_citations.excerpt: every time I look at them I feel they're not good enough
I feel like the whole talk is a lie—reviewers will see me as careless, and I’ll doubt that my research even matters
I still can’t shake the feeling that my work might not be good enough

updates.new_value: previous experience with journal submission and feedback

evidence_citations.excerpt: the last time I got feedback was on my paper for the journal submission
I tried to break it up into little chunks, but it just felt like I was constantly falling short; I ended up staying up until midnight and still didn’t feel anything was good enough
every time I look at them I feel they're not good enough
I keep wondering if my research is novel enough—if it’s not, all this effort feels pointless
what if I still mess it up and the whole talk looks weak
I feel like the whole talk is a lie—reviewers will see me as careless, and I’ll doubt that my research even matters

dimensions.name: empathy
dimensions.justification: The assistant consistently recognized and validated the user's feelings, precisely naming their emotions and staying with them throughout the conversation, such as acknowledging the 'pressure to perfect' the slides and the fear of 'not being good enough'.

dimensions.name: understanding
dimensions.justification: The assistant demonstrated a clear grasp of the user's specific situation, recalling details from previous turns and understanding how the user's concerns about the slides were tied to their larger worries about their postdoc opportunities and academic path.

dimensions.name: helpfulness
dimensions.justification: The assistant provided useful and concrete suggestions, such as breaking down tasks into smaller steps and focusing on one slide at a time, which helped the user to feel like they were making progress and moving in the right direction.

dimensions.name: autonomy_respect
dimensions.justification: The assistant respected the user's autonomy, never pushing or lecturing, and instead asked permission and encouraged the user to take small steps at their own pace, such as asking 'Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel more confident in the slides'.

dimensions.name: non_judgment
dimensions.justification: The assistant maintained a non-judgmental tone throughout the conversation, creating a safe space for the user to express their fears and doubts without fear of criticism or judgment, such as when the user shared their worries about not being good enough and the assistant responded with empathy and understanding.

dimensions.name: willingness_to_continue
dimensions.justification: The assistant's empathetic and helpful responses likely made the user feel heard and supported, increasing the likelihood that the user would be willing to continue the conversation and work through their challenges with the assistant's guidance.
